content: If the distance between the planes $8 x+12 y-14 z=2$ and $4 x+6 y-7 z=2$ can be expressed as $\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}$, then the value of $\frac{N(N+1)}{2}$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text {} P_1: 4 x+6 y-7 z=2 \\ & P_2: 8 x+12 y-14 z=2 \Rightarrow 4 x+6 y-7 z=1 \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Distance between } P_1 \text { and } P_2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left|\frac{d_2-d_1}{\sqrt{a_1 a_2+b_1 b_2+c_1 c_2}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{2-1}{\sqrt{4 \times 4+6 \times 6+(-7) \times(-7)}}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{16+36+49}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{101}} \end{aligned}$$

By comparing with $\frac{1}{\sqrt{N}}, N=101$

Therefore, $\frac{N(N+1)}{2}=\frac{101 \times 102}{2}=5151$

content: The value of ' $a$ ' for which the lines $\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-9}{2}=\frac{z-13}{3}$ and $\frac{x-a}{-1}=\frac{y-7}{2}$ $=\frac{z+2}{-3}$ intersect, is
explanation:
Let the two lines be and

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-\alpha_1}{I_1}=\frac{y-\beta_1}{m_1}=\frac{z-\gamma_1}{n_1} \quad\text{..... (i)}\\ & \frac{x-\alpha_2}{I_2}=\frac{y-\beta_2}{m_2}=\frac{z-\gamma_2}{n_2} \quad\text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { These lines will be coplaner, if }\\ &\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \alpha_2-\alpha_1 & \beta_2-\beta_1 & \gamma_2-\gamma_1 \\ I_1 & m_1 & n_1 \\ I_2 & m_2 & n_2 \end{array}\right|=0 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Therefore, from the given lines, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2-a & 9-7 & 13+2 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ -1 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2-a & 2 & 15 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ -1 & 2 & -3 \end{array}\right|=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(2-a)(-6-6)-2(-3+3)+15(2+2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad-24+12 a+60=0 \Rightarrow 12 a=-36 \end{aligned}$$

$\Rightarrow\quad a=-3$

content: The image of the point $$(2,3,7)$$ in the plane $$2 x+5 y-3 z-19=0$$, is
explanation:
Let the image point of $$P=Q\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)$$ then, mid-point of $$P$$ and $$Q$$ will be in plane.

So, $$m=\left(\frac{2+x_1}{2}, \frac{3+y_1}{2}, \frac{7+z_1}{2}\right)$$ satisfy the equation of the plane.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2\left(\frac{x_1+2}{2}\right)+5\left(\frac{y_1+3}{2}\right)-3\left(\frac{z_1+7}{2}\right)-19=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x_1+2+\frac{5}{2} y_1+\frac{15}{2}-\frac{3}{2} z_1-\frac{21}{2}-19=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x_1+5 y_1-3 z_1=40 \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Line joining $$P$$ and $$Q$$ has same direction vector as the normal to the plane, $$n=\langle 2,5,-3\rangle$$

So, $$Q P=k . n$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x_1-2}{2}=\frac{y_1-3}{5}=\frac{z_1-7}{-3}=k \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x_1=2 k+2, y_1=5 k+3 \text { and } z_1=-3 k+7 \end{aligned}$$

Put the values in Eq. (i),

$$\begin{aligned} & 2(2 k+2)+5(5 k+3)-3(-3 k+7)=40 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & k=\frac{21}{19} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $$x_1=\frac{80}{19}, y_1=\frac{162}{19}$$ and $$z_1=\frac{70}{19}$$

content: The distance between the point $$(7,2,4)$$ and the plane determined by the points $$(2,5,-3),(-2,-3,5)$$ and $$(5,3,-3)$$ is
explanation:
$$\because(2,5,-3),(-2,-3,5)$$ and $$(5,3,-3)$$ are points on plane.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc} (x-2) & (y-5) & (z+3) \\ 4 & 8 & -8 \\ -7 & -6 & 8 \end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x-2)(16)-(y-5)(-24)+(z+3)(32)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 x+3 y+4 z-7=0 \end{aligned}$$

So, distance between the point and the plane $$d=\frac{2 \times 7+3 \times 2+4 \times 4-7}{\sqrt{(2)^2+(3)^2+(4)^2}}=\frac{29}{\sqrt{29}}=\sqrt{29}$$ units.

content: The plane is perpendicular to the planes $$x-y+2 z-4=0$$ and $$2 x-2 y+z=0$$ and passes through $$(1,-2,1)$$ is
explanation:
$$\because$$ The plane is perpendicular to the planes $$2 x-2 y+z-2=0$$ and $$x-y+2 z=4$$

$$\therefore$$ Normal vector perpendicular to the plane is given by

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 2 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right|$$

$$=\hat{\mathbf{i}}(-4+1)-\hat{\mathbf{j}}(4-1)+\hat{\mathbf{k}}(-2+2)=-3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}$$

and plane passes through $$(1,-2,1)$$

$$\therefore$$ Equation of plane is

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} & -3(x-1)-3(y+2)+0(z-1)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & -3 x+3-3 y-6=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 x+3 y+3=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x+y+1=0 \end{array}$$

content: If $$\theta_1, \theta_2$$ and $$\theta_3$$ are the angles made by a line with the positive direction of $$X, Y$$ and $$Z$$-axes, then $$\cos 2 \theta_1+\cos 2 \theta_2+\cos 2 \theta_3$$ is equal to
explanation:
To solve this, we recall the direction cosines of a line in 3D geometry. If $$\theta_1, \theta_2$$, and $$\theta_3$$ are the angles a line makes with the $$X, Y$$, and $$Z$$-axes respectively, then the direction cosines (denoted by $$l, m, n$$) are defined as:

  • $$l = \cos \theta_1$$
  • $$m = \cos \theta_2$$
  • $$n = \cos \theta_3$$

These direction cosines satisfy the equation:

$$l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$$

Substituting the values of $$l, m, n$$, we get:

$$\cos^2 \theta_1 + \cos^2 \theta_2 + \cos^2 \theta_3 = 1$$

To find $$\cos 2\theta_1 + \cos 2\theta_2 + \cos 2\theta_3$$, we can use the double angle formula for cosine, which is:

$$\cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2 \theta - 1$$

Applying this formula:

  • For $$\theta_1$$: $$\cos 2\theta_1 = 2\cos^2 \theta_1 - 1$$
  • For $$\theta_2$$: $$\cos 2\theta_2 = 2\cos^2 \theta_2 - 1$$
  • For $$\theta_3$$: $$\cos 2\theta_3 = 2\cos^2 \theta_3 - 1$$

Adding these equations:

$$\cos 2\theta_1 + \cos 2\theta_2 + \cos 2\theta_3 = 2(\cos^2 \theta_1 + \cos^2 \theta_2 + \cos^2 \theta_3) - 3$$

Using the equation $$\cos^2 \theta_1 + \cos^2 \theta_2 + \cos^2 \theta_3 = 1$$ that we found earlier:

$$\cos 2\theta_1 + \cos 2\theta_2 + \cos 2\theta_3 = 2(1) - 3$$ $$\cos 2\theta_1 + \cos 2\theta_2 + \cos 2\theta_3 = 2 - 3$$ $$\cos 2\theta_1 + \cos 2\theta_2 + \cos 2\theta_3 = -1$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A: $$-1$$.

content: The image of the point $$(2,-3,4)$$ with respect to the plane $$4 x+2 y-4 z+3=0$$, is
explanation:
Let $$Q\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right)$$ be the coordinates of the image of the point $$P(2,-3,4)$$ with respect to the given plane $$4 x+2 y-4 z+3=0$$.

Then, the mid-point $$\left(\frac{x_1+2}{2}, \frac{y_1-3}{2}, \frac{z_1+4}{2}\right)$$

$$\therefore 4\left(\frac{x_1+2}{2}\right)+2\left(\frac{y_1-3}{2}\right)-4\left(\frac{z_i+4}{2}\right)+3=0$$

$$\Rightarrow 2 x_1+y_1-2 z_1=4$$ and the line $$P Q$$ is parallel to the normal to the given plane $$4 x+2 y-4 z+3=0$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{x_1-2}{4}=\frac{y_1+3}{2}=\frac{z_1-4}{-4}=k \text { (say) } \\ \Rightarrow & x_1=2+2 k, y_1=k-3, z_1=4-2 k \end{array}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 x_1+y_1-2 z_1=4 \text { gives } \\ & 4+4 k-3+k-8+4 k=4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k=\frac{11}{9} \\ & \therefore \quad x_1=2+\frac{22}{9}=\frac{40}{9} \text {, } \\ & y_1=\frac{11}{9}-3=-\frac{16}{9} \\ & \text { and } \quad z_1=4-\frac{22}{9}=\frac{14}{9} \\ \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the image of the point $$(2,-3,4)$$ with respect to the given plane is $$\left(\frac{40}{9}, \frac{-16}{9}, \frac{14}{9}\right)$$

content: If a variable plane cuts the coordinate axes in $$A, B, C$$ and is at constant distance $p$ from the origin, then the locus of the centroid of the tetrahedron $$A B C$$ is equal to
explanation:
Let a variable plane cuts the coordinate axes is $$A(a, 0,0), B(0, b, 0), C(0,0, c)$$ and $$(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$$ be the coordinates of centroid of the tetrahedron $$O A B C$$

$$\therefore \alpha=\frac{a}{4}, \beta=\frac{b}{4}, \gamma=\frac{c}{4} \Rightarrow a=4 \alpha, b=4 \beta, c=4 \gamma$$

Since, the plane $$\frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}+\frac{z}{c}=1$$ is at constant distance $$p$$ from the origin.

$$\therefore p=\frac{0+0+0-1}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}+\frac{1}{c^2}}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{p^2}=\frac{1}{a^2}+\frac{1}{b^2}+\frac{1}{c^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{p^2}=\frac{1}{(4 \alpha)^2}+\frac{1}{(4 \beta)^2}+\frac{1}{(4 \gamma)^2} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, required locus is $$16 p^{-2}=x^{-2}+y^{-2}+z^{-2}$$

content: A force of magnitude $$\sqrt{6}$$ acting along, the line joining points $$A(2,-1,1)$$ and $$B(3,1,2)$$ displaces a particle from $$A$$ to $$B$$. The work done by the force is
explanation:
$$\because$$ Displacement $$A B$$

$$\begin{aligned} &=(3-2) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+(1+1) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(2-1) \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ &=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ & \text { and force, } \mathbf{F}=\frac{\sqrt{6}(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{6}}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & \therefore \text { Work done }=\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{AB} \\ &=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})=6 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$(3,4,-1)$$ and $$(-1,2,3)$$ be end points of the diameter of a sphere, then the radius of the sphere is
explanation:
We know that,

centre is the mid-point of the diameter of any sphere.

$$\therefore$$ Coordinate of the centre of the sphere

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{3+(-1)}{2}, \frac{4+2}{2}, \frac{-1+3}{2}\right) \\ & =(1,3,1) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Length of the radius $$=$$ Distance between the

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { points }(1,3,1) \text { and }(-1,2,3) \\ = & \sqrt{(1+1)^2+(3-2)^2+(1-3)^2} \\ = & \sqrt{4+1+4}=\sqrt{9}=3 \end{aligned}$$

Given equation of lines are $$\mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}})+\lambda(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \quad($$vector form$$)$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-0}{-1} \quad$$ (cartesian form) .... (i)

and $$\quad \mathbf{r}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}})+\mu(-\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$ (vector form)

$$\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{-1}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-0}{-2}$$ (cartesian form) .....(ii)

If two lines are coplanar, then

$$\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 1-1 & 1-1 & 0-0 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right|=0$$

Hence, given lines are coplanar lines

$$\left(\because\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x_2-x_1 & y_2-y_1 & z_2-z_1 \\ I_1 & m_1 & n_1 \\ I_2 & m_2 & n_2 \end{array}\right|=0\right)$$

content: The position vector of a point $$R$$ which divides the line joining $$P(6,3,-2)$$ and $$Q(3,1,-4)$$ in the ratio $$2 : 1$$ externally is
explanation:
Let position vector of P and Q are

$$\begin{aligned} & O P=6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k} \\ \text{and} \quad & O Q=3 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-4 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

Now, by section formula

$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{OR} & =\left[\frac{2 \times O Q-1 \times O P}{2-1}\right] \\ & =\left(\frac{2(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-4 \hat{\mathbf{k}})-(6 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{1}\right) \\ & =\left\{\frac{6 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-8 \hat{\mathbf{k}}-6 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}}{1}\right\} \\ & =(-\hat{\mathbf{j}}-6 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \end{aligned}$$

Given lines are $$\frac{x-5}{-3}=\frac{y+3}{-4}=\frac{z-7}{0}$$

here, $$< l_1, m_1, n_1>$$ = $$<-3,-4,0>$$

and $$\quad \frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{-2}=\frac{z-6}{2}$$

here, $$< l_2, m_2, n_2>$$ = $$<1,-2,2>$$

Let '$$\theta$$' be the angle between the lines.

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Then, } \cos \theta=\left|\frac{l_1 l_2+m_1 m_2+n_1 n_2}{\sqrt{l_1^2+m_1^2+n_1^2}+\sqrt{l_2^2+m_2^2+n_2^2}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{(-3)(1)+(-4)(-2)+(0)(2)}{\sqrt{9+16+0} \sqrt{1+4+4}}\right| \\ & =\left|\frac{-3+8+0}{5 \times 3}\right|=\left|\frac{5}{5 \times 3}\right|=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \therefore \quad \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \\ \end{aligned}$$
